title: Evaluation Metrics for NLP
concept: Standard classification metrics like F1-score and confusion matrices are essential, but sequence-generation tasks need specialized overlap scores.

exercises.prompt: When do we need specialized sequence-generation metrics instead of standard classification metrics?

options.text: When classifying photos of cats and dogs.
When generating new text sentences, like in translation.
When checking if a number is positive or negative.

exercises.explain: When models generate creative, variable text, standard 'yes/no' metrics are too rigid. We need metrics that measure phrase and word overlaps.

cards.title: Grading a Creative AI
cards.body: If you build an AI that writes summaries or translates languages, how do you grade it? A simple 'pass/fail' grade doesn't work because there are a hundred different ways to write the same correct sentence! We need smart metrics that can grade creative text.

cards.title: Classification vs. Generation Metrics
cards.body: Classification metrics (like F1-score) check if we got a simple label right or wrong. Sequence-generation metrics (like BLEU or ROUGE) check how much of the generated text overlaps with a human-written reference, measuring similarity rather than strict correctness.

cards.title: The Translation Judge
cards.body: Imagine you are judging a translation contest. Instead of checking if the contestants wrote the exact same words as your answer sheet, you count how many matching words and phrases they used. If they generated the same ideas, they get a high score. NLP metrics do the same thing: they act like speed-judges counting overlapping phrases!

cards.title: Getting Partial Credit
cards.body: If a model translates a sentence to "The puppy runs" and the human reference is "The dog runs", a classification check sees this as 100% incorrect because the strings don't match. But a sequence-generation metric checks the overlapping words and gives partial credit because "runs" and "The" match!

cards.title: Myth: Only One Right Answer
cards.body: People often think that if an AI translation doesn't match the human reference perfectly, it is wrong. In reality, multiple different translations can be perfect! Overlap metrics help us measure quality even when the wording differs.

cards.title: Choosing the Right Tool
cards.body: See how classification metrics compare with sequence-generation metrics when evaluating model outputs.

right.text: Checks word similarity and overlap. Good for Translation and Summaries.

left.text: Checks if a label matches exactly. Good for True/False or Categorizing.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks is best suited for a traditional classification metric like F1-score?

options.text: Classifying emails as either spam or inbox.
Summarizing a long news article into one sentence.
Generating a creative story from a prompt.

exercises.explain: F1-score works well when classifying items into categories (like Spam or Not Spam). It doesn't work well for grading open-ended generated paragraphs.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Standard classification metrics are the best way to evaluate if a chatbot's paragraphs sound natural and accurate.
exercises.explain: Standard classification metrics check for exact label matches, whereas sequence-generation metrics look at matching words or word groups.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence about evaluating generated text.
exercises.sentence: Instead of looking for a single correct label, we measure word [blank] to evaluate generated sentences.
exercises.explain: Because there are many correct ways to write a sentence, we look at how much the AI's words overlap with human-written sentences to grade them.

exercises.prompt: Identify the output from a sequence-generation evaluation metric.

options.text: Teacher: 'Your translation is beautiful and uses great vocabulary!'
System: 'Checking text similarity... 82% of word combinations match the reference.'

exercises.explain: The second response is generated by an AI evaluation system using a specialized metric, as it calculates a percentage score based on matching phrases. The first is a human grader, and the third is a classification label.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need specialized sequence-generation metrics (like word-overlap scores) instead of just using standard classification accuracy for tasks like translation?

options.text: They measure accuracy faster by ignoring spelling mistakes.
Generative tasks have many correct ways to write a sentence, requiring word-overlap checks.
Classification metrics only work on numerical data, not text data.
Generative tasks do not use training data, so they need special scores.

exercises.explain: Generative NLP tasks (like writing a story or translating a sentence) have many valid correct answers. Traditional classification metrics look for rigid 'yes/no' labels, whereas generation metrics look for overlapping words and phrases to measure quality flexibly.
